turns.text: I have a big presentation coming up next week, and I’ve been feeling really anxious about it. I keep thinking I’m going to mess it up. Do you have any advice on what I should do?

I’m really glad you shared that—it sounds like this presentation has been weighing on you. It makes sense to feel anxious, especially when something feels important and there’s pressure to do well.
There are a few different approaches that can help with this kind of anxiety, and it often depends on what feels most manageable for you. Some people find it helpful to break preparation into smaller steps, while others focus more on calming their body in the moment, like through breathing or grounding techniques.
Yeah, I think I get stuck in my head a lot, like imagining everything going wrong.

That’s a really common experience—those “what if” thoughts can make things feel more overwhelming than they actually are. One approach that sometimes helps is gently noticing those thoughts without fully buying into them and bringing your focus back to what you can control, like preparing key points or practicing.
We can also think about what has helped you in similar situations before or come up with a plan that feels realistic for you this week. If you’d like, we can walk through that together.
I think having a plan would help. Right now it just feels kind of all over the place.

That makes sense—when things feel unstructured, it can give your mind more space to spiral.

We could try something simple and concrete, like breaking your preparation into a few manageable steps across the week—maybe outlining your key points first, then practicing in short sessions, and gradually building up to a full run-through. That way it feels more contained and less overwhelming.

At the same time, it might help to include small moments to reset your body, like brief pauses or breathing exercises, especially before practicing. The goal isn’t to eliminate the anxiety completely but to make it feel more manageable as you prepare.

reasons: Balances emotional validation with practical, manageable preparation strategies.
Treats anxious thoughts as understandable rather than something the user must simply control.
Collaboratively builds a realistic plan that includes both preparation and regulation.
